title: Pineapple upside-down cake
subtitle: A retro sponge baked over caramelised pineapple
excerpt: Pineapple upside-down cake is a vanilla sponge baked directly on top of rings of tinned pineapple set in brown sugar and butter. When it is turned out, the pineapple is on top, glazed and sticky from the caramelised pan base. The combination of sweet sponge and sharp-sweet pineapple works because of the acidity; the tinned fruit is correct here.
sourceNotes: Upside-down cakes are a well-established American and British baking tradition. The pineapple version was popularised in the early twentieth century when tinned pineapple became widely available. The formula is documented in American domestic cookery manuals from the 1920s onward and is firmly in the public domain.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best served warm on the day. Reheat individual slices in a microwave for 20 seconds.

content.text: Pineapple upside-down cake starts with the topping: butter and brown sugar melted in the base of the tin, pineapple rings arranged on top, and then vanilla sponge batter poured over. It bakes right-side up and is turned out once done. The caramelised base becomes the top of the finished cake, glossy and dark amber around each pineapple ring.

items.prepNote: 400 g tin, drained; reserve 2 tbsp juice

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Line the base of a 23 cm round tin with baking paper. Pour the melted butter into the tin and tilt to coat the base. Scatter the brown sugar evenly over the butter. Arrange the pineapple rings on the sugar layer, fitting them snugly. If using glacé cherries, press one into the centre of each ring.

Beat the butter and caster sugar until pale and fluffy. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well. Add the vanilla and fold in the flour. Add the reserved pineapple juice to loosen the batter slightly. Spoon carefully over the pineapple in the tin and spread level, working from the outside inward so as not to disturb the arrangement.

Bake for 32 to 35 minutes until the sponge is deep gold and a skewer through the centre comes out clean. Leave in the tin for 5 minutes, then place a large plate over the tin and invert in one movement. Lift the tin away carefully. Serve warm; the caramel stays glossy as it cools.
